content.text: Energy-Based Models

An energy-based model (EBM) assigns a scalar energy E(x) to each configuration x. Lower energy means higher probability — the model says that plausible configurations settle into low-energy states, just as physical systems tend toward configurations of minimum potential energy. The probability distribution is:

Z is the partition function — a normalising constant that sums (or integrates) over all possible configurations. For binary vectors of length d, Z is a sum over 2ᵈ terms. This is the core difficulty: Z is generally intractable, which is why approximate inference (MCMC, variational methods) dominates EBM training.
Boltzmann Distribution

The form P ∝ exp(−E) is the Boltzmann (or Gibbs) distribution from statistical mechanics, where E is the energy and the temperature T controls the spread. In machine learning, T is usually absorbed into the energy or set to 1:
T→0: probability concentrates on the minimum-energy configuration (argmax).
T→∞: distribution approaches uniform — all configurations equally likely.
T=1: the standard machine-learning regime; the energy directly sets log-probabilities up to a constant.
Boltzmann Machine Energy
A Boltzmann machine is a network of binary stochastic units v ∈ {0,1}ⁿ with pairwise interactions. Its energy function is:

attrs.latex: E(\mathbf{v}) = -\sum_{i} b_i v_i - \sum_{i < j} W_{ij} v_i v_j

content.text: where bᵢ are per-unit biases and Wᵢⱼ are symmetric pairwise connection weights. A positive Wᵢⱼ means units i and j prefer to be in the same state (both on or both off); a negative Wᵢⱼ means they prefer opposite states.

import numpy as np

def boltzmann_energy(v, b, W):
    """
    v: binary state vector (n,)
    b: bias vector (n,)
    W: weight matrix (n, n), symmetric, zero diagonal
    Returns: scalar energy
    """
    bias_term      = -b @ v
    interaction_term = -0.5 * v @ W @ v   # 0.5 avoids double-counting i<j
    return bias_term + interaction_term

# Simple 3-unit example
n = 3
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
W = rng.normal(0, 0.5, (n, n))
W = (W + W.T) / 2   # symmetrize
np.fill_diagonal(W, 0)
b = rng.normal(0, 0.5, n)

# Enumerate all 2^n=8 configurations and compute their energies
from itertools import product as iproduct
configs = np.array(list(iproduct([0, 1], repeat=n)))
energies = np.array([boltzmann_energy(v, b, W) for v in configs])

# Convert to probabilities via softmax over -energies
log_probs = -energies - np.log(np.exp(-energies).sum())
probs = np.exp(log_probs)
for cfg, p in zip(configs, probs):
    print(f"{cfg} → E={boltzmann_energy(cfg,b,W):.2f}  P={p:.4f}")
Why Energy Functions Are Expressive

The energy function can be any real-valued function — there is no requirement for it to factorise, normalise, or satisfy any conditional independence property. This means energy-based models can represent complex multimodal distributions, including distributions with long-range correlations that directional graphical models (Bayes nets) cannot represent without intractable inference.

The trade-off is the partition function Z. In a directed model, Z=1 by construction (probabilities sum to 1 along each conditional). In an EBM, Z must be explicitly computed or approximated at training time, which is where methods like contrastive divergence, MCMC, and score matching come in.
The Log-Likelihood Gradient
Training an EBM by maximising the log-likelihood of a dataset {x⁽ⁿ⁾} gives a gradient that is the difference of two expectations:

The first term (the negative data energy gradient) is easy — it just requires evaluating the energy at the observed data point. The second term (the model expectation) requires averaging over samples from the model distribution P — this is the intractable part. The contrastive divergence algorithm approximates this second term with a short MCMC chain started from a data point.
Connection to Modern Models

Energy-based thinking recurs throughout deep learning. A softmax classifier assigns scores (negative energies) to each class and normalises with a partition function over the class set. Score matching — used in diffusion model training — avoids computing Z by matching the gradient of the log-density (the score ∇x log P(x)) rather than the density itself. Neural network potentials for molecular simulation are EBMs over atom configurations. The energy function perspective unifies these as a single framework: define an energy, define a distribution via the Boltzmann transform, and approximate learning using one of the available partition-function-free estimators.
Architecture — Energy-Based Model Overview

attrs.alt: Energy-Based Model — energy landscape, partition function, and training methods

content.text: Why Energy Functions Matter

Energy functions provide a principled way to define probability distributions without requiring normalisation to be computed explicitly. This is a profound advantage: normalising a distribution over a high-dimensional space requires summing or integrating over all possible configurations, which is exponentially hard in the dimensionality. By parameterising the unnormalised log-probability (the negative energy), energy-based models can define flexible distributions over arbitrary structured spaces — images, molecular configurations, sequences — without any tractability constraint on the model family.

In the context of generative AI, energy functions underpin diffusion models, which are currently the dominant paradigm for image generation. Score matching — the objective used to train score-based diffusion models — avoids computing the partition function by learning the gradient of the log-density (called the score), which is exactly
. This connection makes EBM theory directly relevant to understanding modern text-to-image systems like Stable Diffusion.
Energy functions also appear in discriminative models: a softmax classifier with logits (pre-softmax scores)
defines a joint energy
and a marginal energy (free energy)
. This perspective connects classification to the EBM framework and motivates energy-based out-of-distribution detection.
Benefits of Energy-Based Models
No normalisation constraint on the model.
E(x) can be any real-valued function — deep network, Gaussian process, polynomial. Normalisation is handled implicitly via the partition function.
Natural representation of multimodal distributions.
Energy landscapes with multiple minima naturally represent distributions with multiple modes, which are difficult to model with unimodal parametric distributions.
Applicable to structured output spaces.
EBMs can define distributions over graphs, sequences, configurations, and other structured spaces without the normalisation difficulty that affects directed graphical models.
Unified framework.
Softmax classifiers, RBMs, Boltzmann machines, diffusion models, and normalising flows can all be viewed through the EBM lens, enabling knowledge transfer across model families.
Compositionality.
Multiple energy functions can be added:
corresponds to multiplying (and renormalising) the two distributions, enabling modular constraint combination.
Trade-offs
Intractable partition function.
Computing Z requires summing over exponentially many states (discrete) or an intractable integral (continuous). Approximate inference is mandatory.
Sampling is expensive.
Generating samples from an EBM requires MCMC (Langevin dynamics, Metropolis-Hastings, Gibbs sampling), which can require thousands of steps for high-dimensional data.
Training instability.
Contrastive divergence approximations introduce bias; the model distribution can collapse or diverge if hyperparameters are poorly chosen.
Mode coverage vs. mode seeking.
MCMC-based EBM training can miss modes of the distribution, leading to models that cover some but not all of the data distribution.
Slow inference.
Unlike VAEs or normalising flows which have closed-form sampling, EBM sampling requires iterative MCMC, making them slow at inference time.
When to Use Energy Functions
Energy-based models are the right choice in these situations:
Undirected dependencies.
When modelling mutual constraints between variables where no natural causal ordering exists — e.g., image completion, structured prediction with circular dependencies.
Composing constraints.
When multiple independent constraints must be simultaneously satisfied — e.g., a molecule that is simultaneously stable, soluble, and has a specific binding pocket.
Out-of-distribution detection.
The free energy of a classifier naturally separates in-distribution from out-of-distribution inputs: in-distribution inputs have lower free energy.
Score-based generation (diffusion).
Denoising score matching trains a neural network to estimate the gradient of the log data density, which is the score of an EBM. Use diffusion models instead of explicit EBMs for generation.
When NOT to use explicit EBMs:
When fast sampling is required.
If generation speed at inference is critical, use a normalising flow or VAE — they provide exact, fast sampling.
When the partition function has a closed form.
If the model family admits a tractable normalising constant (e.g., Gaussian, categorical), use a directed probabilistic model instead.
How to Implement
The following implements an energy-based model trained with Langevin MCMC and contrastive divergence:

import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim


# ── 1. Energy function (simple MLP) ────────────────────────────────────────
class EnergyNet(nn.Module):
    """
    A simple MLP that maps input x → scalar energy E(x).
    Lower energy = higher probability under the EBM.
    """

    def __init__(self, input_dim: int, hidden_dim: int = 128):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1),
        )

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """x: (B, D) → energy: (B,)"""
        return self.net(x).squeeze(-1)


# ── 2. Langevin MCMC sampling ───────────────────────────────────────────────
def langevin_sample(
    energy_fn: nn.Module,
    x_init: torch.Tensor,
    n_steps: int = 60,
    step_size: float = 0.01,
    noise_scale: float = 0.005,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Unadjusted Langevin dynamics:
        x_{t+1} = x_t - (step_size/2) * ∇_x E(x_t) + noise
    Returns samples after n_steps steps.
    """
    x = x_init.clone().detach().requires_grad_(True)
    for _ in range(n_steps):
        energy = energy_fn(x).sum()
        grad   = torch.autograd.grad(energy, x)[0]
        with torch.no_grad():
            x = x - (step_size / 2) * grad
            x = x + noise_scale * torch.randn_like(x)
        x = x.detach().requires_grad_(True)
    return x.detach()


# ── 3. Contrastive Divergence training ─────────────────────────────────────
def train_ebm(
    data: torch.Tensor,
    input_dim: int = 2,
    n_epochs: int = 100,
    batch_size: int = 64,
    lr: float = 1e-3,
    cd_steps: int = 60,
) -> EnergyNet:
    """
    Maximise log-likelihood via contrastive divergence:
      ∂L/∂θ = -∇E(x+) + ∇E(x-)
    where x+ are data samples and x- are model (MCMC) samples.
    """
    model = EnergyNet(input_dim)
    opt   = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)

    for epoch in range(n_epochs):
        idx = torch.randperm(len(data))[:batch_size]
        x_pos = data[idx]                               # real data

        # Initialise MCMC from noise
        x_neg_init = torch.randn_like(x_pos)
        x_neg = langevin_sample(model, x_neg_init, n_steps=cd_steps)

        # Contrastive divergence loss
        # Minimise E(x+) and maximise E(x-) → push data to low energy
        e_pos = model(x_pos).mean()
        e_neg = model(x_neg).mean()
        loss  = e_pos - e_neg

        opt.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        # Gradient clipping for stability
        torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=0.1)
        opt.step()

        if epoch % 20 == 0:
            print(f"Epoch {epoch:4d} | E_pos={e_pos.item():.3f} | E_neg={e_neg.item():.3f} | loss={loss.item():.3f}")

    return model


# ── 4. Demonstration on 2D Gaussian mixture ─────────────────────────────────
if __name__ == "__main__":
    rng = torch.manual_seed(42)

    # True data: mixture of two Gaussians in 2D
    n = 512
    data_a = torch.randn(n // 2, 2) * 0.4 + torch.tensor([-2.0, 0.0])
    data_b = torch.randn(n // 2, 2) * 0.4 + torch.tensor([ 2.0, 0.0])
    data   = torch.cat([data_a, data_b])

    # Train EBM
    model = train_ebm(data, input_dim=2, n_epochs=100, cd_steps=40)

    # Evaluate: data points should have lower energy than random noise
    test_data  = data[:20]
    test_noise = torch.randn(20, 2) * 5
    print(f"\nMean energy (data):  {model(test_data).mean().item():.3f}")
    print(f"Mean energy (noise): {model(test_noise).mean().item():.3f}")
    # Data energy should be lower (more probable)
Examples
Example 1: Restricted Boltzmann Machine with visible and hidden units
An RBM is a bipartite EBM where visible units v model data and hidden units h capture latent features:
Example 2: Energy-based out-of-distribution detection

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F


def free_energy(logits: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Free energy (Helmholtz free energy) of a classifier:
        F(x) = -log sum_k exp(f_k(x))
    Lower F(x) → in-distribution. Higher F(x) → out-of-distribution.

    This is the logsumexp of logits with a negative sign.
    Reference: Liu et al. (2020) "Energy-based Out-of-distribution Detection"
    """
    return -torch.logsumexp(logits, dim=1)


# Simulate a trained classifier
class ToyClassifier(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim=10, n_classes=5):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(in_dim, 64), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(64, n_classes)
        )
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)


model = ToyClassifier(in_dim=10, n_classes=5)

# In-distribution: unit Gaussian
x_in  = torch.randn(100, 10)
# Out-of-distribution: far from origin
x_ood = torch.randn(100, 10) * 8 + 5

with torch.no_grad():
    logits_in  = model(x_in)
    logits_ood = model(x_ood)
    energy_in  = free_energy(logits_in)
    energy_ood = free_energy(logits_ood)

print(f"In-distribution  mean energy: {energy_in.mean():.2f} ± {energy_in.std():.2f}")
print(f"Out-of-distribution mean energy: {energy_ood.mean():.2f} ± {energy_ood.std():.2f}")
print("\nThreshold (e.g., -20): flag inputs with energy > threshold as OOD")
# After training, OOD inputs consistently have higher (less negative) energy.
Real-World Example

Diffusion models — the architecture behind Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, and Imagen — are deeply connected to energy-based models through score matching. The following illustrates the connection between score matching and the EBM framework:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim


class ScoreNetwork(nn.Module):
    """
    A score network estimates ∇_x log p(x) = -∇_x E(x).
    In diffusion models, this is a noise-conditioned score network s_θ(x, σ).
    """

    def __init__(self, dim: int = 2, hidden: int = 128):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(dim + 1, hidden),  # +1 for noise level σ
            nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),
            nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, dim),      # output: gradient in input space
        )

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor, sigma: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Returns estimated score ∇_x log p_σ(x)"""
        sigma_emb = sigma.unsqueeze(-1).expand(x.shape[0], 1)
        return self.net(torch.cat([x, sigma_emb], dim=-1))


def denoising_score_matching_loss(
    score_net: ScoreNetwork,
    x_clean: torch.Tensor,
    sigma_min: float = 0.01,
    sigma_max: float = 1.0,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Denoising Score Matching (DSM):
    L = E_σ E_x E_ε [ ||s_θ(x+ε, σ) - (-ε/σ²)||² ]

    The optimal score at noise level σ is -ε/σ², where ε ~ N(0, σ²I).
    This avoids computing the partition function entirely.
    """
    B = x_clean.shape[0]
    # Sample noise levels from log-uniform distribution
    log_sigma = torch.rand(B) * (torch.log(torch.tensor(sigma_max)) -
                                  torch.log(torch.tensor(sigma_min))) + torch.log(torch.tensor(sigma_min))
    sigma = torch.exp(log_sigma)                         # (B,)
    noise = torch.randn_like(x_clean) * sigma.unsqueeze(1)
    x_noisy = x_clean + noise                            # (B, D)

    # Estimated score
    s_hat = score_net(x_noisy, sigma)                    # (B, D)
    # True score at this noise level: -noise/σ²
    s_true = -noise / (sigma.unsqueeze(1) ** 2)          # (B, D)

    # Weighted MSE (weight by σ² to equalise contribution across noise levels)
    weight = sigma.unsqueeze(1) ** 2
    return (weight * (s_hat - s_true) ** 2).mean()


# Quick training demo on 2D Gaussian mixture
if __name__ == "__main__":
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    data_a = torch.randn(256, 2) * 0.3 + torch.tensor([-1.5, 0.0])
    data_b = torch.randn(256, 2) * 0.3 + torch.tensor([ 1.5, 0.0])
    data   = torch.cat([data_a, data_b])

    score_net = ScoreNetwork(dim=2)
    opt       = optim.Adam(score_net.parameters(), lr=1e-3)

    for step in range(300):
        idx   = torch.randperm(len(data))[:64]
        loss  = denoising_score_matching_loss(score_net, data[idx])
        opt.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        opt.step()
        if step % 100 == 0:
            print(f"Step {step:3d} | DSM loss: {loss.item():.4f}")

    print("\nScore network trained. Use Langevin dynamics with this score to generate samples.")
Common Mistakes
Using the partition function in the loss.
Do not attempt to compute Z directly. The entire point of contrastive divergence, score matching, and NCE is to avoid computing Z.
Too few MCMC steps in CD.
CD-1 (one Gibbs step) introduces significant bias into the gradient estimate. Use CD-k with k≥10, or persistent CD (persistent Markov chains).
Unconstrained energy output.
If E(x) can grow without bound, the partition function may not converge. For continuous distributions, ensure the energy goes to +∞ as ∥x∥→∞.
Ignoring gradient clipping.
EBM training can produce large gradient spikes. Always clip gradients during training (max_norm=0.1 to 1.0 is common).
Conflating score and energy.
The score is
— the negative gradient of the energy, not the energy itself. Score matching trains to match this gradient, not E(x) directly.
Best Practices
Prefer score matching for continuous distributions.
Score matching avoids computing Z entirely and produces stable gradients. Denoising score matching (DSM) is the most practical variant.
Use spectral normalisation for the energy network.
Spectral normalisation constrains the Lipschitz constant of E(x), preventing energy values from growing too large and destabilising training.
Initialise MCMC chains from data for CD.
Starting MCMC from data rather than noise reduces the number of steps needed to reach the model distribution, improving CD gradient quality.
Monitor energy gap.
Track the energy of data samples versus model samples (random noise) during training. A widening gap (data energy decreasing, noise energy increasing) indicates the model is learning.
Use persistent chains (PCD) for better mixing.
Persistent CD maintains a buffer of MCMC samples between training steps, allowing chains to mix more thoroughly than restarting from scratch each batch.
Performance and Scalability

The fundamental scalability challenge for EBMs is MCMC. Langevin dynamics requires O(T) forward passes and gradient evaluations per sample, where T is the number of steps needed for the chain to mix. For high-dimensional data (images), T can be in the hundreds or thousands, making EBM training 100-1000× more expensive than likelihood-based models like VAEs.

Score-based diffusion models solve this by using a hierarchy of noise levels, allowing short Langevin chains at each noise level to collectively approximate the score function. This amortised approach scales to high-resolution images and is the reason diffusion models are tractable while naive EBMs are not.

Security and Reliability

Energy-based models face specific reliability challenges in production. MCMC-based sampling is sensitive to the energy landscape geometry: a landscape with many deep local minima separated by high barriers (multimodal distributions) causes Langevin chains to become trapped in individual modes, resulting in models that generate diverse training samples but cannot explore the full distribution at inference time.

From a security perspective, the free energy of a classifier provides a natural OOD detector, but it can be fooled by adversarial inputs that have been crafted to have low energy despite being semantically meaningless. This is because adversarial examples lie in the low-energy manifold of the learned model while being far from the true data manifold.
Mode collapse in EBM training:
Monitor sample diversity during training. If all MCMC samples converge to one mode, reduce the step size or increase CD-k.
Adversarial OOD:
Free energy OOD detection can be defeated by adversarially optimising inputs to minimize energy while remaining semantically OOD.
Numerical instability in MCMC:
Large step sizes in Langevin dynamics produce divergent chains. Use gradient clipping and adaptive step sizes.
Key Takeaways
An energy function
maps configurations to scalars; lower energy means higher probability under the Gibbs distribution
The partition function Z normalises the distribution but is generally intractable; approximate inference (MCMC, score matching, variational) is required.
Boltzmann machines are a special case of EBMs with binary units, biases, and pairwise weights; they define the energy as
The log-likelihood gradient decomposes into a data term (easy) and a model expectation term (intractable); contrastive divergence approximates the second term with short MCMC.
Score matching avoids Z entirely by matching
; diffusion models are the practical realisation of this idea at scale.
Energy functions can be composed additively to represent joint constraints, enabling modular generative models.
Free energy of a classifier — the negative logsumexp of logits — is a powerful out-of-distribution detection signal.
EBM training is slow due to MCMC; diffusion models have replaced explicit EBMs for most generative applications while retaining the theoretical framework.
